food_subgroup: Noodle dishes — dry tossed noodles with plant-based protein

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mala Kolomee. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class. Dry tossed noodle with plant-based protein and mala spice blend is not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM (Noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for mala-spiced dry kolomee with soy mince and fried yuba in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific dish. SNOMED CT is not applicable for this food record.

main_ingredients: mala sauce (Sichuan peppercorn, dried chilli, spices, oil)
fried yuba (tofu skin / bean curd skin)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of dry mala kolomee with minced soy and fried yuba, approximately 380 g, as typically served at a Singapore restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Noodles, egg, cooked (FDC ID 172030); Textured soy protein, dry (FDC ID 174276); Tofu, fried (FDC ID 172448); Mala sauce component estimated from chilli oil and Sichuan spice blend analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for Dry Mala Kolomee with Minced Soy and Fried Yuba. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked wheat noodles (~55% by weight), rehydrated textured soy protein (~15%), fried tofu skin/yuba (~15%), and mala sauce/oil (~15%). Proportions are based on typical restaurant-style dry kolomee construction. Energy and macros are cross-checked against comparable Singapore vegan noodle dish profiles. Values represent a best-estimate per 100 g of the combined dish as served.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Wheat noodles, boiled (GI ~52, glucose reference).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dry Mala Kolomee with Minced Soy and Fried Yuba. GI of 52 is estimated from boiled wheat noodles (GI ~52, glucose reference) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The mala sauce, soy protein, and fried yuba components are expected to lower the effective GI due to added fat, protein, and fibre, making GI 52 a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving = (52 × 22.5 × 3.80) / 100 = 44.5 g (serving size 380 g, carbohydrate 22.5 g per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1824 mg per serving) from mala sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load per serving (~1824 mg), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; if consumed, portion size should be strictly reduced and no additional salty condiments added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content with low GI (estimated ~52) partially offset by high fat and protein from soy and yuba
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low estimated GI (~52) and moderate protein content from soy mince and fried yuba may support a slower postprandial glucose rise, but the large serving size (380 g) delivers approximately 85.5 g of carbohydrate per bowl, requiring careful carbohydrate counting and portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from mala sauce and fried yuba; plant-based protein profile is cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the plant-based protein sources (soy mince and yuba) offer a cardioprotective lipid profile with no cholesterol and low saturated fat, the high sodium content and calorie-dense mala sauce represent cardiovascular risk factors that warrant portion moderation for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Mala Kolomee with Minced Soy and Fried Yuba is a fully vegan, spicy Singaporean-Chinese fusion noodle dish served at specialty restaurants in Singapore. The dish is built on kolomee — a thin, springy wheat-based noodle of Sarawakian-Chinese origin — tossed dry (without broth) in a bold mala sauce made from Sichuan peppercorns, dried chillies, chilli oil, garlic, sesame oil, and soy sauce. The mala sauce delivers the characteristic numbing-spicy (麻辣) flavour profile that has become popular in Singapore's contemporary dining scene. The protein components are entirely plant-based: minced soy protein (textured vegetable protein, TVP) provides a meaty texture and substantial protein content, while fried yuba (腐皮, tofu skin or bean curd skin) adds a rich, chewy, slightly crispy element with additional soy-derived protein and fat. The dish is finished with spring onion and sesame oil. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 198 kcal per 100 g, with 9.2 g protein, 8.5 g fat, and 22.5 g carbohydrate. The estimated glycemic index of ~52 (low GI) reflects the wheat noodle base moderated by the fat and protein from soy and yuba components. Sodium content is high (~480 mg per 100 g), primarily from the mala sauce and soy sauce seasoning, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is free of cholesterol and dairy, and is suitable for vegans. It is not suitable for individuals with gluten or soy allergies. Clinically, the moderate GI and high plant protein content are positive attributes for glycaemic management, but the high sodium and calorie-dense sauce require portion awareness for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Dry Mala Kolomee with Minced Soy and Fried Yuba exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients (cooked wheat noodles, textured soy protein, fried tofu skin, mala sauce analogue). GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (boiled wheat noodles, GI ~52) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is systematic and defensible but not based on direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is available in any approved primary source. 2. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component USDA entries used for this composite estimate and cannot be defensibly assigned. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the plant-based components used; plant-based noodle dishes are not a meaningful source of vitamin D. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the component USDA entries and cannot be defensibly assigned for this composite. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the primary components used. 6. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully vegan dish containing no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 7. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is 0.0 as plant-based foods do not naturally contain vitamin B12; naturally zero for this vegan dish. 8. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in this dish; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 9. GI of 52 is a conservative analogous estimate from boiled wheat noodles; actual GI may be lower due to fat and protein from mala sauce, soy mince, and fried yuba. 10. Sodium content is high and represents a primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. 11. The dish is served at Flavours by Saute - Funan, Singapore (field survey data source). 12. Halal status is not confirmed; mala sauce ingredients and preparation method require halal certification verification.
